coredata.dc:title: Novel strategies for enhancing energy metabolism and wastewater treatment in algae-bacteria symbiotic system through carbon dots-induced photogenerated electrons: The definitive role of accelerated electron transport

coredata.dc:description: The nitrogen removal efficiency of the algae-bacteria symbiotic system (ABSS) remains challenging due to competition for light capture and insufficient electron donors in aquaculture wastewater. To overcome this bottleneck issue, we first identified and reported the introduction of photogenerated electrons into ABSS by coupling them with carbon dots (CDs). The energy metabolism (21.52 %-56.25 %) and pollutant degradation efficiency (89.36 %-96.42 %) of ABSS/CDs surpassed the biochemical limit observed in traditional ABSS. Notably, the addtion of CDs facilitated an opening of the plastoquinone-pool (PQ-pool) valve and accelerated electron transfer rates, enabling photogenerated electrons to selectively target light-harvesting complexs as activation sites, thereby mobilizing photosynthesis and respiratory system activity to enhance nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide reduced (NADH) and adenosine triphosphate (ATP) production while promoting tricarboxylic acid (TCA) and Calvin cycle processes. In simulated swine wastewater treatment, ABSS/CDs achieved higher removal rates for the chemical oxygen demand (COD), total nitrogen (TN) and ammonium-nitrogen (NH4 +-N) (100 %, 99.23 % and 98.62 % respectively) compared to pure culture systems alone. Network-based analysis revealed that ABSS/CDs improved microbial community structure stability within the photobioreactor while demonstrating efficient coupling between fungi, nitrifying bacteria, and denitrifying bacteria for enhanced performance in nitrogen removal processes. The upregulation of nitrogen metabolism and PQ-pool genes maintains efficient electron transport rates and contributes to nitrogen removal. This study expands our understanding of the physiological aptitudes of ABSS and provides valuable insights into applying CDs photoelectrons for enhanced wastewater treatment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Construction of symbiotic system between bacteria and algae (a and b) transcriptomic analysis reveal systematic changes in the regulation of KEGG pathway enrichment at algal and bacterial levels in ABSS state and (c) IAA and Trp metabolism and nitrogen removal in the construction of ABSS.

Effects of CDs on photosynthetic efficiency and central carbon metabolism including (a) electron transport of photosynthetic pathway, (b) expression of the photosynthetic genes, (c) central carbon metabolism, (d) maximum potential quantum efficiency (Fv/Fm), (e) actual quantum efficiency of PSII photochemistry (YII), (f) relative electron transport rate (rETR) and (g) non-photochemical quenching (NPQ). The results are presented as the percentage compared with each control as averages with standard deviations (n = 3). A, B, C and D stands for A, ABSS, A + 5 mg/L CDs and ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs groups. The inset shows the log2 colorcoding scale. The numbers above the error bars represents significant difference (p < 0.05, 0.01, or 0.001, t-test, respectively).

CDs regulates nitrogen removal rate and energy supply by changing the electron transport capacity and electron consumption of the respiratory chain. (a) electron transport in the respiratory chain; (b) TCA, EMP and HMP cycle; (c) changes in the expression levels of differentially expressed genes encoding NADH dehydrogenase, succinic acid/fumarate reductase, Cyt bc1 complex, Cyt c complex, ATP synthase and essential enzymes at each step of the denitrification process; (d) the changes of ATP contents, (e) the changes of NADH contents and (f) ETSA levels under different treatment conditions. The results are presented as the percentage compared with each control as averages with standard deviations (n = 3). The inset shows the log2 colorcoding scale. The symbol (*, ** or ***) above the error bars represents significant difference (P < 0.05, 0.01, or 0.001, t-test, respectively).

Topological networks with highly connected modules (≥3 nodes) and Z-P plots of the different treatment groups. The color of the nodes represents the main phyla. The node size is proportional to its relative abundance in the microbial community. The red line represents a positive correlation and the blue line represents a negative correlation. The node in the middle of the module is the module hub, and the node in the red circle is the connector. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

ABSS/CDs regulates the core genes changes in PBR. (a) Linear regression models between core nitrogen cycle genes and α diversity in four treatment groups. (b) Procrustes analysis of the microbial abundance profile and core nitrogen cycle genes in ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs group. (c) Correlation matrix of the related genes of nitrogen cycle and PQ-pool and core microbial in ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs group. Line color represents the level of statistical significance, and line thickness represents the Mantel’s r statistic value for the associated distance correlations.
Integrated mechanism of CDs-induced improvement for ABSS metabolism, electron transport and nitrogen removal efficiency.

head.ce:title: Novel strategies for enhancing energy metabolism and wastewater treatment in algae-bacteria symbiotic system through carbon dots-induced photogenerated electrons: The definitive role of accelerated electron transport

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Resources and Environment, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin 150030, Heilongjiang, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Resources and Environment, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin 150030, Heilongjiang, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nitrogen removal efficiency of the algae-bacteria symbiotic system (ABSS) remains challenging due to competition for light capture and insufficient electron donors in aquaculture wastewater. To overcome this bottleneck issue, we first identified and reported the introduction of photogenerated electrons into ABSS by coupling them with carbon dots (CDs). The energy metabolism (21.52 %-56.25 %) and pollutant degradation efficiency (89.36 %-96.42 %) of ABSS/CDs surpassed the biochemical limit observed in traditional ABSS. Notably, the addtion of CDs facilitated an opening of the plastoquinone-pool (PQ-pool) valve and accelerated electron transfer rates, enabling photogenerated electrons to selectively target light-harvesting complexs as activation sites, thereby mobilizing photosynthesis and respiratory system activity to enhance nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide reduced (NADH) and adenosine triphosphate (ATP) production while promoting tricarboxylic acid (TCA) and Calvin cycle processes. In simulated swine wastewater treatment, ABSS/CDs achieved higher removal rates for the chemical oxygen demand (COD), total nitrogen (TN) and ammonium-nitrogen (NH4 +-N) (100 %, 99.23 % and 98.62 % respectively) compared to pure culture systems alone. Network-based analysis revealed that ABSS/CDs improved microbial community structure stability within the photobioreactor while demonstrating efficient coupling between fungi, nitrifying bacteria, and denitrifying bacteria for enhanced performance in nitrogen removal processes. The upregulation of nitrogen metabolism and PQ-pool genes maintains efficient electron transport rates and contributes to nitrogen removal. This study expands our understanding of the physiological aptitudes of ABSS and provides valuable insights into applying CDs photoelectrons for enhanced wastewater treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Algae-bacteria symbiotic system (ABSS) processes have gained significant global attention due to their potential in treating high nitrogen concentration wastewater, such as aquaculture wastewater and livestock waste, while simultaneously offering the dual benefits of bioremediation and sustainable biomass production [1–3]. However, the practical application of ABSS faces challenges arising from its low stability and efficiency, which are largely influenced by the distinct responses and material exchange dynamics between algae and bacteria under varying environmental conditions [4,5]. Among these challenges, light energy plays a pivotal role in modulating ABSS activity and nutrient absorption [6,7]. Firstly, competition for light capture occurs between algae and photosynthetic bacteria (PSB), given their overlapping niches; however, algae tend to outcompete PSB for light resources [8–10]. Secondly, the assimilation of residual carbon by algae leads to a deficiency of electron donors that impedes metabolite accumulation and chemical signal transmission [11–13].

The plastoquinone-pool (PQ-pool) is at the “crossroads” of the linear electron transport chain with other electron transport chains in the photosynthetic system and is generally considered to be the electron carrier of photosynthesis [14–17]. The redox state of the PQ-pool seems to play a pivotal role in sensing cellular energy status and in regulating photosynthetic capacity [18]. It was found that the kinase triggered by the electron transfer rate of the PQ-pool not only regulates state transition but also regulates the activity of transcription factors and retrograde signaling [19]. Moreover, it has been reported that over-reduction of the PQ-pool leads to the downregulation of photosystems (PS) II and consequent degradation of the entire photosynthetic mechanism [20]. Meanwhile, the electron transport from PQ-pool can accelerate nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate reduced (NADPH) production for the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) and Calvin cycle accumulation of biomass, which is a conserved energy metabolism and carbon fixation pathway in microorganisms [17,19]. Hence, it is reasonable to assume that under the competitive condition of light capture in ABSS, the PQ-pool serves as a modulator for light flux initiation within ABSS, thereby restraining the electron transfer rate. Overcoming the bottleneck of associated with this speed limit electron transfer represents a crucial way to enhance stability, energy metabolism, and nitrogen removal efficiency in ABSS. However, to our knowledge, no relevant studies have been conducted.

Integrating nanomaterials with microorganisms has emerged as an efficient approach to enhance biological processes harnessing light energy [21,22]. Nanomaterial-generated photoelectrons can serve as readily available reducing equivalents, thereby augmenting metabolic pathways [23,24]. Consequently, incorporating the photoelectric properties of nanomaterials into ABSS holds great potential for revolutionizing electron transport within the system and driving it toward high-performance outcomes across diverse species [25–27]. This innovative method may not only address the competition for light harvesting within ABSS but also accelerate energy metabolism, facilitate chemical signal exchange, and increase the nitrogen removal rate [4]. However, there remains a scarcity of relevant studies. Among the various nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) not only generate photoelectrons as light collectors but also serve as electron shuttles due to their surface electron absorption and electron donor functional groups [28–30]. This dual role may facilitate the generation of additional electrons for the electron transport chain, thereby improving its efficiency [31]. Hu et al. [32] revealed that the CDs modification of acetogenic bacteria enhanced the extracellular electron transfer and the formation of electroactive biofilm, which subsequently improved acetate production from CO2 in microbial electrosynthesis. Xue et al. [29] discovered that the CDs significantly promoted Chlorella growth by ∼15 %. Meanwhile, potential intracellular bioresources, pigments, carbohydrates, proteins, and lipids were generally enhanced. Therefore, we propose that the ABSS/CDs coupled system could effectively address the issue of low efficiency of the rate-limiting step by accelerating the electron transporter, thereby augmenting the downstream electron transfer rate. Essentially, the response mechanism and electron transport behavior of ABSS to photoelectrons generated by CDs are intricately linked to ABSS’s energy metabolism, chemical signal exchange, and nitrogen removal efficiency. In previous studies, most researchers only focused on the effects of CDs on single bacteria or single algae, and did not fully analyze the competitive relationship between photosynthetic microorganisms (microalgae and PSB) in the natural environment [29]. In addition, most of the relevant studies adopted the “black box” research method, only focusing on the change of indicators, and did not clarify the impact of the electron transfer process between CDs and microorganisms on the metabolic pathway [24]. This has prevented a comprehensive assessment of the effects of electron deficiency on ABSS under natural conditions.

In this study, ABSS and CDs have been identified for the first time as pivotal components for constructing photoactive biotic photosynthetic systems and facilitating electron transfer between species through photoelectron strategies. We used high-throughput sequencing and transcriptomics data at a certain of sequencing depths to (1) investigate the performance and interaction mechanism of ABSS/CDs coupling system, encompassing energy metabolism, chemical signal exchange, and nitrogen removal efficiency; (2) elucidate the key action sites and regulatory mechanisms governing the limiting steps of CDs in triggering photoelectrons in ABSS; (3) demonstrate the contribution of CDs-directed electron transfer to enhance photosynthesis and respiration in ABSS, promoting material metabolism and improving nitrogen removal efficiency; (4) explore the effects of ABSS/CDs coupling system on water quality, the dynamic changes of microbial community structure, and metabolic functions in wastewater treatment. This research is expected to provide novel insights into harnessing nanomaterial potential for enhancing microbial functionality.

The microalgae and bacteria utilized in this study were obtained from indigenous wastewater, specifically Parachlorella kessleri sp. HP9 and Delftia acidovorans sp. YP3. The cultivation of these microorganisms was conducted under simulated swine wastewater conditions, maintaining a temperature of 25 ± 0.5 ℃. The cultivation environment also included an illumination with a light intensity of 200 μmol/(m2·s) and followed a photoperiod of 12/12 h light/dark [33]. The composition of simulated swine wastewater is provided in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cds synthesis, characterization and concentration screening
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, corn stalk powder was utilized as precursors in a straightforward procedure for the preparation of CDs particles. The CDs with tunable emission could directionally shift un-utilized light at wavelengths of 500–600 nm to blue light to promote ABSS photosynthesis. The CDs could sustainably provide optimized irradiation for biomass production. We elaborated characterization of CDs, specifically their contributions to improving biomass production, bioenergy generation, photosynthesis and electron transport. Further details on the synthesis and characterization of CDs can be found in Text S1 of Supporting Information, while Text S2 describes the screening tests conduct to determine CDs concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement of ABSS growth, intracellular components, photosynthetic activity and nitrogen removal efficiency by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The single algae culture and ABSS cultures were supplemented with CDs, at a dosage of 5 mg/L on day 0, referred to as A + 5 mg/L CDs groups and ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs groups, respectively. Meanwhile, the single algae culture and ABSS were treated without CDs (referred to as A groups and ABSS groups) under identical conditions. Consistent with the experimental design described in Section 2.1, the investigation encompassed microbial growth, biomass production, nutrient uptake, photosynthetic activity, and oxidative stress indicators. Further details regarding this procedure can be found in Texts S3 and S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and operation of ABSS/CDs in simulated swine wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: Four ABSS microcosms were established in a photobioreactor (PBR) with dimensions of 60 cm height and 30 cm diameter (Fig. S1). Subsequently, a 5 % inoculum consisting of HP9 and YP3 suspensions at a ratio of 1:1, along with CDs at a concentration of 5 mg/L, was added to the PBR [34,35]. The wastewater used for cultivation was simulated swine wastewater (Table S1). Specific water quality and environmental parameter tests were assessed as described in Text S5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical analysis and electron transport system activity
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed information regarding electrochemical analysis and electron transport system activity can be found in Text S6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microbial community and transcriptomic sequencing and analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To capture the dynamic sequence of photogenerated electrons in response to microbial species’ CDs in an ABSS system, it is imperative to perform longitudinal sampling and sequencing of microbial samples throughout the experiment [36]. The details of 16S/18S rRNA gene diversity sequencing and transcriptomic sequencing are provided in Text S7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General statistical analysis and data visualization
ce:section.ce:para: The primary statistical analyses and visualizations were conducted using customized Tutools platform, Origin 2023, Gephi 0.9.2, and FAPROTAX (v1.2). Further details can be found in Text S8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of symbiotic system between bacteria and algae
Mechanisms of specific strategies for ABSS
ce:section.ce:para: Under the synergistic effect of ABSS, indoleacetic acid (IAA) and tryptophan (Trp), as key signaling molecules of photosynthetic bacteria and microalgae, play a crucial role in ABSS metabolism and nitrogen removal efficiency. Further details can be found in Text S9.

The presence of differentially expressed genes (DEGs) between the A and ABSS groups was confirmed using volcano maps (Fig. S2), indicating that the growth and internal physiology of HP9 and YP3 were complex. KEGG pathway enrichment analysis was conducted for DEGs, revealing significant enrichment in amino acid metabolism, carbohydrate metabolism, energy metabolism, lipid metabolism, and secondary metabolism pathways at the algae expression level, as depicted in Fig. 1 a. Specifically, up-regulation was observed in Trp biosynthesis, TCA cycle, Calvin cycle, nitrogen metabolism, lipid metabolism, and fatty acid biosynthesis pathways. Furthermore, at the bacterial expression level shown in Fig. 1b, up-regulated DEGs were identified in the TCA cycle, nitrogen cycle, and IAA biosynthesis pathways.

Subsequently, we investigated the metabolism of Trp and IAA as well as the specific mechanism of nitrogen removal between algae and bacteria in the ABSS (Fig. 1c). In algal cells, there is an up-regulation of transcriptional abundance for most key enzymes involved in the Calvin cycle, while IAA can be transported into algal cells through efflux pumps and converted to Trp under the influence of the Calvin cycle (Fig. S3a) [37–39]. Additionally, once bacteria receive Trp secreted by algae, bacterial cells participate in endogenous up-regulation of Trp biosynthesis genes along with specific enzyme genes (Fig. S3b), thereby facilitating IAA biosynthesis. These findings suggest that both IAA and Trp serve as signal molecules promoting growth and metabolism within ABSS [40]. Nitrogen is effectively removed by algae mainly through the reduction of NO3 – and NO2 –, while NH4 + is directly converted to amino acids via the glutamine synthase-glutamate synthase pathway (Fig. 1c). The expression of glnA, gltB/D, gdhA, and GDH2 associated with nitrogen removal was upregulated under ABSS conditions in both algal and bacterial cells (Fig. S3). Furthermore, the introduction of bacteria enhanced the symbiotic system’s capacity for nitrogen absorption by facilitating signal exchange through the TCA cycle, which accelerates nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide reduced (NADH) energy input into the nitrogen cycle and improves the overall efficiency of nitrogen removal.

Notably, the expression levels of PQ-pool electron transport, photosynthesis, oxidative phosphorylation, ABC transporter, and ATP synthesis coupled electron transport pathways were down-regulated in algae and bacteria (Fig. 1a and b). As depicted in Figs. 2 a and S4a, the PQ-pool serves as a crucial node within the cyclic electron transport (CET) of algae and PSB. It plays a pivotal role in the linear electron transport chain as well as other electron transport chains by acting as an essential carrier for both photosynthesis and respiration processes [41]. Weliwatte et al. [42] discovered that the rate-limiting step for electron flow occurs upstream of where electrons enter the PQ-pool, rather than between Cyt b6/f and photosystem I (PSI). Among these steps, the respiratory chain plays a role in facilitating electron transfer from the matrix-reducing agent to oxygen through PTOX and mdaB, thereby enhancing electron transfer between PSI and photosystem II (PSII) functionally [43]. As depicted in Fig. S4b and c, compared to group A, gene expression levels of PTOX and mdaB decreased by 21.71 % and 19.63 %, respectively, in ABSS samples. This suggests that HP9 and YP3′s inhibition on electron transfer in ABSS is primarily influenced by insufficient upstream electrons at the rate-limiting step PQ-pool, leading to overall energy metabolism inhibition.

Fig. 2b demonstrates that all transcripts encoding PSI subunits, as well as PSII subunits exhibited down-regulation by 1.03–2.54-fold. Notably, the parameters of PetA and PetD involved in CET were also down-regulated. This gene expression was primarily regulated by PQ-pool activity which may be associated with PQ-pool inhibition, reduced electron transfer within the Cyt b6 /f complex and Cyt bc1 (Fig. S4) [37]. The variation of four typical fluorescence parameters in the photosystem is depicted in Fig. S5. Compared to the A group, the ABSS group exhibited a 20.23 % reduction in the maximum photochemical efficiency of maximum potential quantum efficiency (Fv/Fm), while actual quantum efficiency (YII) and relative electron transport rates (rETR) showed comparable or slightly increased values. Moreover, the increase in nonphotochemical quenching (NPQ) elucidates the additional energy consumption resulting from competition among ABSS [22]. These findings demonstrate that under ABSS conditions, electron transfer is constrained by light capture, leading to reduced PSII activity due to diminished electron transfer within the PQ-pool.

In summary, a symbiotic mechanism of signal exchange (Trp and IAA) exists in ABSS to regulate cell growth, energy accumulation, and nitrogen removal. However, there is competition for light capture between HP9 and YP3, which hinders the rate of electron transfer in the PQ-pool and becomes the limiting step for electron transfer in the photosynthetic system. Therefore, further investigation is required to identify strategies that can enhance PQ-pool activity and accelerate electron transfer rates in ABSS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cds leads to enhanced material metabolism and nutrient uptake in ABSS
ce:section.ce:para: To overcome the limitation of electron transfer in the PQ-pool, this study postulates that incorporating nanomaterials capable of generating photoinduced electrons could augment the electron transfer rate and subsequently enhance the energy accumulation and nutrient absorption performance of ABSS. Consequently, CDs were synthesized from waste soybean straw to investigate the impact of coupling the ABSS/CDs system on the ameliorating rate-limiting step and low electron transfer rate in ABSS. Texts S10 and S11 provide additional information on CDs related studies.

The findings revealed that CDs induced minimal oxidative stress in microorganisms, and a concentration of 5 mg/L CDs exhibited a stabilizing and protective effect on the cell membranes of ABSS (Text S12). Additionally, this study demonstrated that a concentration of 5 mg/L CDs promoted the growth and metabolism of HP9 and YP3 strains (Text S13).

Under the influence of CDs, the intracellular lipid content increased by 56.25 % compared with ABSS alone in the ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs group (Fig. S6). Furthermore, there was a significant increase in saturated fatty acid (SFA) levels by 22.61 % and 14.82 %, respectively (Table S2). Conversely, unsaturated fatty acid (UFA) content decreased by 15.11 % and 15.43 %, respectively, ruling out CDs-induced oxidative stress [44,45]. Notably, no statistically significant difference in protein content among the four treatment groups was observed (p > 0.05), indicating that the addition of CDs did not lead to excessive protein accumulation to resist stress response [46]. Compared to the CK group, intracellular carbohydrate content increased significantly by 42.64 % and 51.83 %, respectively, in the CDs groups. Moreover, CDs positively influenced carotenoids and chlorophyll accumulation likely through enhanced electron transport rate promoting pigment biosynthesis [47]. Compared to the CK group, Trp content increased by 13.27 % when A + 5 mg/L CDs were added, while both IAA and Trp contents of ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs increased by 21.52 % and 26.11 %, respectively. These findings indicate that the addition of CDs effectively enhances signal molecular transduction between ABSS species and elevates the levels of IAA and Trp.

The chemical oxygen demand (COD) removal rates of A, ABSS, A + 5 mg/L CDs， and ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs treatment groups were 51.33 %, 87.35 %, 64.82 %, and 96.42 %, respectively, as depicted in Fig. S6. These results indicate that a significant competition for organic carbon sources between HP9 and YP3 strains [4]. The total nitrogen (TN) removal rates for the A + 5 mg/L CDs and ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs groups exhibited significant increases, reaching 61.19 % and 89.36 %, respectively (p < 0.05). This indicates that CDs played a crucial role in the consumption of organic carbon sources and nitrogen. Moreover, the ammonium-nitrogen (NH4 +-N) removal rates of A + 5 mg/L CDs and ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs were higher by 24.63 % and 32.74 %, respectively compared to those of A (41.28 %) and ABSS (60.15 %). Notably, the achieved NH4 +-N removal rate in this study surpassed the previously reported value of 87.13 % [48]. Additionally, both A + 5 mg/L CDs and ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs treatments demonstrated nitrogen (NO3 –-N) removal rates that were significantly higher by factors of approximately 1.57 and 2.83-fold compared to those observed with A treatment alone or ABSS treatment alone, respectively. Thus, it can be concluded that CDs serve as essential electron donors which greatly enhance energy metabolism efficiency as well as nutrient removal capability of ABSS through improved PQ-pool activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cds regulates photosynthesis and affects the regeneration of metabolites
ce:section.ce:para: Characterizing CDs-induced transcriptional changes in HP9 and YP3, which are the main exposure sites for ABSS and exhibit the highest sensitivity to CDs, will provide comprehensive mechanistic insights into the regulation of CDs with a high level of detail [49]. The unsupervised principal component analysis (PCA) score plot demonstrated a significant separation between the CK group and the groups treated with CDs at 10 d (Fig. S7), indicating substantial transcriptional changes in both HP9 and YP3 following exposure to CDs. Analysis of DEGs (adjusted p < 0.05, |log2FC| > 1) through the volcano maps revealed that the addition of CDs significantly altered the expression of DEGs in both HP9 and YP3 (p < 0.05, Fig. S8a–d).

The KEGG functional enrichment analysis revealed that the addition of CDs activated pathways associated with photosynthesis, PQ-pool electron transport, ABC transporter, and ATP synthesis coupled electron transport (Fig. S8e and f). As depicted in Fig. 2a, the introduction of CDs effectively harnessed solar energy to generate electrons, thereby providing an ample supply for HP9 and YP3 systems. This enhanced electron transport within the PQ-pool, facilitated efficient electron input into light-harvesting complex I (LHI), light-harvesting complex II (LHII), PI and PSII complexes, consequently activating the photosynthetic system activity of ABSS [13,18]. In Fig. 2b, it can be observed that the transcription expression levels of light-harvesting complex (LHC) genes were up-regulated in response to CDs treatment. Furthermore, all transcripts encoding PSI photosynthetic protein subunits exhibited a significant increase ranging from 1.53 to 2.68-fold. These findings indicate that the addition of CDs effectively mitigated the inhibition of electron transfer during light capture. Notably, there was a significant up-regulation (1.55–3.58-fold) in gene expression related to PQ-pool function (PTOX and mdaB) upon CDs treatment (Fig. S4). This suggests that CDs act as efficient electron donors capable of overcoming limitations of PQ-pool dynamics; thus promoting efficient electron transfer to ABSS resulting in heightened photosynthetic system activity [17].

Fig. 2c depicts the DEGs involved in central carbon metabolism, which represents the most streamlined and optimized biochemical pathway for cell growth and energy supply in metabolic networks [50]. Notably, acetyl-CoA, a crucial regulator maintaining the adequate metabolic flow of fatty acids, exhibited significantly increased activity driven by pdhA, acyl, and ACSS genes with fold changes of 274.24 %, 42.23 %, and 81.26 % in the ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs group (p < 0.05). These findings indicate a substantial enhancement in fatty acids metabolic activity. Simultaneously, the entry of acetyl-CoA into TAG metabolism led to significant up-regulation of all genes involved in triglyceride synthesis [45]. Proteins, consisting of chains of amino acids, play a crucial role in primary metabolism [51]. Consequently, the expression levels of specific genes (SHMT, GS, and proB) involved in precursor amino acid biosynthesis were found to be 1.3–3.7-fold higher, resulting in a gradual increase in protein abundance. This observation suggests a consistent metabolic adaptation to CDs regulation [52]. Moreover, glycolytic flux was augmented in the CDs group due to significant up-regulation of HK, a “gatekeeper” of starch metabolism that exhibited an increase of 22.52 % and 38.41 %. Additionally, genes associated with starch synthesis (glgA, glgB, and glgC) were also up-regulated. Furthermore, key genes involved in carotenoid biosynthesis (LUT1, ZEP and crtZ) were up-regulated by 47.32 %, 52.53 %, and 44.72 %, respectively; potentially leading to elevated total carotenoid levels.

Notably, the PQ-pool served as an electron transfer mediator between photosynthetic system transport and the downstream TCA cycle, facilitating energy production in the form of NADH and ATP [53]. Additionally, pyruvate metabolism connects various biological metabolites to the TCA cycle, enhancing electron transfer efficiency [54]. It was observed that the reprogramming pyruvate utilization through electron transfer via the PQ-pool resulted in more efficient acetyl-CoA production (Fig. 2c). In the ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs group, up-regulation of key genes involved in TCA cycle (CS, aco and icdh) by 53.61 %, 18.22 % and 67.41 %, respectively, ensured rapid electron flux across the biochemical network for NADH and ATP regeneration [55,56]. Therefore, acting as a critical “gas station” in the biological electron transfer process, the PQ-pool facilitates rapid electron transfers and promotes energy metabolism as well as nutrient removal [57].

Compared to the CK group, the addition of CDs resulted in a significant increase in Fv/Fm values by 33.32 % and 41.34 %, respectively (Fig. 2d). These values remained above 0.3, indicating a healthy state and normal photosynthesis process [58]. The Y(II) and rETR values, commonly used as indicators of electron transport efficiency and photosynthetic efficiency of PSII, were comparable or slightly higher compared to the A and ABSS groups (Fig. 2e and f) [22]. Furthermore, the reduction in NPQ observed in the CDs group suggested a decrease in excess energy expenditure for activating PSII anti-stress protection (Fig. 2g) [59], which aligned with the findings on reduced reactive oxygen species (ROS) production and oxidase activity mentioned earlier in Section 3.2.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on microbial nitrogen removal
ce:section.ce:para: The addition of CDs activated the “oxidative phosphorylation pathway” according to KEGG functional enrichment analysis (Fig. S8e and f). Subsequent investigation into gene expression alterations related to nitrogen removal and electron transport chain (ETC) was presented in Fig. 3 a. Microbial metabolism primarily relies on electron transport and energy metabolism, which determine the efficiency of nitrogen removal [54]. The introduction of CDs triggered a “domino effect” in the ETC, facilitating electron transfer from NADH dehydrogenase to complex III, Cyt c, and associated denitrogenase enzymes. This resulted in enhanced electron movement within the PQ-pool and up-regulation of PQ-pool related genes (Fig. S4) [60]. Additionally, the Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas (EMP) glycolytic pathway and hexose monophosphate pathway (HMP) that utilize glucose as their primary carbon source were promoted, leading to up-regulated expression of relevant genes, which played a cruial role in COD removal (Fig. S6) [61]. The TCA cycle utilized phosphoenolpyruvate (a metabolite of the EMP pathway) as its carbon source, providing both electron donors (NADH) and energy carriers (ATP) for efficient electron transport and consumption within the respiratory chain (Fig. 3b) [62].

In the ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs group, there was an increase in the level of dehydrogenase responsible for NADH oxidation (Fig. 3c). Differential expression was observed in genes encoding Nuo, with up-regulation of NuoA by 57.36 %, which catalyzed NADH dehydrogenation. Analysis using the NADH test kit showed a 21.62 ± 8.41 % increase in NADH content compared to the control group (Fig. 3d), suggesting accelerated electron transfer and maintenance of a highly reductive environment [63]. Activations were also observed in both NADH dehydrogenase and succinate dehydrogenase, responsible for electron transfer in ETC (Fig. 3c) [64]. Up-regulation of the genes encoding for NuoB and NuoC (30.31 %-41.57 %) might enhance electron output participation, while activated Sdh facilitated rapid electron passage through ETC. The significant up-regulation of Cyt bc1 complex gene (34.52 %-58.94 %) promoted electron release via coenzyme Q, whereas Cyt c oxidase had its gene up-regulated by 1.34–3.11-fold supporting that CDs did not cause oxidative stress response (Fig. S9) [65]. Significant differential expression of ATP synthase located in ETC was also observed (Fig. 3c). Meanwhile, ATP and NADH contents were 52.6 ± 10.3 % and 27.3 ± 8.4 % higher than those in the control group (Fig. 3d and e). Furthermore, the activities of complex I, III, and ETSA increased by 19.5 ± 8.7 %, 17.5 ± 8.7 %, and 39.5 ± 8.7 %, respectively; these complexes play crucial roles as mediators in electron transport (Fig. 3f and S10). These results suggested that the activity of complex I directly affected NADH content and electron production, ultimately promoting overall electron transfer [66].

To further validate the enhancement of nitrogen removal efficiency in ABSS by the addition of CDs, enzyme activity assays were performed. The results demonstrated that in the ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs group, there was a significant increase in the activities of AMO and NXR, with increments of 29.37 ± 8.42 % and 39.19 ± 7.92 %, respectively, compared to the CK group (Fig. S11). Moreover, the relative abundances of genes encoding AmoA/B/C and NxrA/B/K were elevated by approximately 2.53–3.48-fold (Fig. 3c). Fig. S11 depicted that in ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs group, there was an up-regulation observed for NIR, NAR, NOS and NOR activities by approximately 21.63 ± 3.22 %, 20.42 ± 7.59 %, 35.71 ± 6.95 % and 26.14 ± 6.86 %, respectively. These findings corresponded to a notable increase in relative abundances ranging from 2.17 to 3.62-fold for genes encoding NirK/S, NarI/H/G/K, NosZ, and NorB/C in the ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs group (Fig. 3c). This enhancement can be attributed to the activation of PQ-pool by CDs, where NADH generates a substantial number of electrons through complex I. Subsequently, these electrons are transferred to key enzymes via electron transport chains, thereby increasing enzyme activity [67–69]. In summary, CDs act as a “booster” facilitating efficient electron transfer and consumption, which is crucial for maintaining the stability of microbial nitrogen removal functions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ABSS/CDs modulates the pollutant removal performance, microbial community succession and core genes alterations in a simulated swine wastewater system

ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the impact of ABSS/CDs coupling system on pollutant removal in wastewater, an 85 days experiment was conducted. During the initial cycle of PBR operation (1–30 days), the effluent exhibited reductions to 0 mg/L for COD, 12.6 mg/L for NH4 +-N and 0.28 mg/L for TN with the addition of ABSS/CDs (Fig. S12). This observation suggests microorganisms stored the influent’s carbon source internally. Additionally, some loss of TN may be attribute to ammonia assimilation by microalgae [2,34]. In a typical dark-light cycle, dissolved oxygen (DO) concentrations rises and falls repeatedly, thereby further enhancing nitrifying and denitrifying bacteria activity (Fig. S13) [70,71]. These findings demonstrate that this system can facilitate both nitrification and denitrification through symbiotic interactions between microalgae and bacteria.

As the experiment progressed and PBR operation stabilized (31–85 days), the ABSS/CDs group exhibited complete removal rates of COD, 98.62 % removal rate of NH4 +-N, and 99.23 % removal rate of TN. These findings highlight the significant role played by ABSS/CDs in facilitating PBR operation. The introduction of ABSS/CDs may have disrupted previously stable microbial communities, leading to intricate microalgae-bacterial interactions. ABSS/CDs may provide a large number of electrons and affect interactions between bacterial communities, further affecting system performance [64,66].

The community pattern consistently indicated the dominance of ABSS in the wastewater treatment process (Fig. S14). Further details are described in Text S14. To gain a deeper understanding of the interrelationships among microorganisms in sewage, we constructed a topological network diagram (Fig. 4 ). Compared to A and ABSS (40 and 59 nodes), A + 5 mg/L CDs and ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs exhibited more than 91 and 200 network nodes respectively, suggesting that the addition of CDs enhances the competitive advantage of key microorganisms [25]. Moreover, the network of the CDs group displayed greater proximity and complexity compared to that of the control group. Positive and negative connections demonstrated an increasing trend in the CDs group, with the negative correlation ratio increased to 53.13 % and 58.31 %, respectively (Fig. 4a–d). This indicated that highly connected nodes played a crucial role in ensuring network robustness, with increased negative correlations associated with more stable and resilient microbial communities [72,73].

The key microorganisms forming microbial ecological networks were identified based on their intra-module connectivity (Zi) and inter-module connectivity (Pi) (Fig. 4e–h) [74]. In this study, compared to group A, the network of group A + 5 mg/L CDs exhibited two module hubs originating from different phyla: Chlorophyta and Basidiomycota, respectively. Additionally, six connectors were detected including Basidiomycota, Cyliofrana, Ascomycetes, and Proteobacteria (Fig. 4f). Therefore, the addition of A + 5 mg/L CDs primarily enhanced small and medium-sized algae communities or even rare algae communities in wastewater to maintain the stability of micro-environmental ecological niches [67]. Compared to the ABSS group, the network of the ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs group exhibited an increased number of module hubs and connectors. Notably, among these, Chlorophyta and Proteobacteria contributed 5 module hubs and 6 connectors. Meanwhile, it underscores the dominance of Chlorophyta and Proteobacteria within this ecological niche (Fig. 4h). In summary, ABSS/CDs have the ability to modify both structural complexity and diversity within wastewater niches and ultimately determine functional potential in these intricate microbial ecosystems [75].

ce:section.ce:section-title: ABSS/CDs regulates the core genes changes in PBR
ce:section.ce:para: The contribution of α diversity to the change of core genes in four different treatment groups was investigated using linear regression analysis. The results revealed strong positive correlations were observed between α diversity and N cycling genes in the A + 5 mg/L CDs (r = 0.97, p < 0.001) and ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs (r = 0.95, p < 0.001) groups (Fig. 5 a). These findings indicate that CDs plays a crucial role in regulating the distribution of nitrogen cycle genes during wastewater treatment. Further validation through Procrustes analysis demonstrated a significant correlation specifically between α diversity and N cycling genes specifically in the ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs group (M2 = 0.6539, P = 0.002) (Fig. 5b), reinforcing the notion that ABSS/CDs effectively regulate microbial diversity within their original ecological niche and enhance N cycling efficiency in wastewater.

The Mantel test assessed the impact of four core microorganisms (Proteobacteria, Bacteroidota, Chlorophyta, and Basidiomycota) on nitrogen cycle and PQ-pool related genes in the ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs group. As depicted in Fig. 5c, nitrogen cycle genes in the ABSS + 5 mg/L CDs group exhibited significant positive correlations with the four core microorganisms (p < 0.05). Similarly, PQ-pool related genes also displayed positive associations with these microorganisms, particularly emphasizing a strong connection with Proteobacteria. Previous research has demonstrated that Proteobacteria and Bacteroidota play pivotal roles as mobile elements associated with the abundance of nitrification and denitrification genes in wastewater treatment [76]. Furthermore, Chlorophyta and Basidiomycota have been found to harbor nitrogen removal related genes, highlighting their significance in nitrogen removal processes. Notably, both Proteobacteria and Chlorophyta commonly possess AOX and PTOX enzymes which are crucial for enhancing electron transfer efficiency during wastewater treatment [77,78]. In conclusion, Proteobacteria, Bacteroidota, Chlorophyta, and Basidiomycota emerge as the principal drivers of nitrogen metabolism pathway module and PQ-pool function in wastewater treatment under the regulation of ABSS/CDs. The incorporation of ABSS/CDs effectively circumvents the rate-limiting step associated with PQ-pool in wastewater treatment, thereby expediting electron transfer among core microorganisms and consequently enhancing energy accumulation and nitrogen metabolism rates.

The stability of ABSS was maintained through the signal exchange in pure culture; however, its low electron transfer rate poses a barrier due to the limitation of the PQ-pool. The addition of CDs overcame this bottleneck and induced an increase in metabolism, electron transport, and nitrogen removal efficiency (Fig. 6 ). Initially, CDs circumvented the rate-limiting step “PQ-pool” in PSB and algae by harnessing photo-induced electrons, thereby boosting electron transfer within the photosynthetic system, enhancing the TCA and Calvin cycle, and accelerating material accumulation and exchange (21.52 %-56.25 %). Secondly, CDs stimulated electron flow in the respiratory chain while facilitating EMP pathway, HMP pathway, and nitrogen metabolic pathways resulting in improved COD and nitrogen uptake (89.36 %-96.42 %). Lastly, CDs mitigated oxidative stress as evidenced by reduced ROS levels and suppressed antioxidant enzyme activities. These metabolic enhancements collectively fostered ABSS proliferation, growth, and nitrogen removal efficiency.

Notably, CDs played a pivotal role in orchestrating the dynamics of microbial community assembly in response to varying wastewater conditions, particularly by facilitating electron transfer rates through unlocking the PQ-pool valve. This heightened electron flux activates numerous microbial activities, fostering the emergence of a more cohesive microbial community within PBR (Fig. 4). Simultaneously, the up-regulation of genes associated with nitrogen removal and PQ-pool functions in PBR enhanced removal efficiencies of COD, TN, and NH4 +-N (100 %, 99.23 % and 98.62 %, respectively), facilitated by increased availability of electrons (Figs. 3 and 5). These changes in microbial communities corresponded with alterations in their functional attributes, highlighting the critical role of establishing a resilient and metabolically active microbial community for optimizing efficiency in removing pollutants from wastewater.

The present study introduces a novel approach utilizing CDs to concurrently stimulate the activities of ABSS and enhance nutrient uptake from wastewater. This innovative strategy effectively addresses challenges related to electron competition and low ABSS activities in wastewater treatment. Among them, CDs had good electron conductivity and could be used as electron mediators to promote rapid electron transfer and efficient energy transfer in the photosynthesis and respiration systems of ABSS, which help reduce energy loss in the electron transport chain and enhance the activity of metabolic pathways of ABSS [79]. Meanwhile, ABSS had the ability to produce more valuable metabolites and biodegradation, and using the enhanced light absorption capacity of CDs could significantly improve the accumulation of energy substances and the efficiency of nitrogen removal processes [80,81]. By integrating the proposed ABSS/CDs coupled system into real-world wastewater scenarios, valuable insights can be gained regarding the contributions of evolved indigenous microorganisms towards optimizing wastewater treatment using ABSS-based technologies. Furthermore, understanding electron transport within ABSS/CDs systems will facilitate the design of more effective photoactive biohybrids for environmental applications. However, further research is necessary to optimize these technologies and evaluate their long-term environmental impacts and benefits associated with CDs in ABSS applications.

This study revealed the mechanisms through which the CDs influenced the energy metabolism and nitrogen removal capacity of ABSS. Specifically, the addition of CDs facilitated an opening of the PQ-pool valve and accelerated electron transfer rates, enabling photogenerated electrons to selectively target light-harvesting complexes as activation sites, thereby mobilizing photosynthesis and respiratory system activity to enhance proliferation, energy metabolism, and nitrogen removal efficiency of ABSS. Notably, in simulated swine wastewater treatment, ABSS/CDs activated microbial activities and improved microbial community structure stability within PBR, while demonstrating efficient coupling between fungi, nitrifying bacteria, and denitrifying bacteria for enhanced performance in nitrogen removal processes. Meanwhile, the up-regulation of nitrogen metabolism and PQ-pool gene maintains an efficient electron transport rate, which contributes to the removal of COD, TN, and NH4 +-N.

Chi Zhang: Writing – original draft. Mingwei Zhou: Data curation. Yiming Zhang: Conceptualization. Dapeng Li: Validation. Ning Hou: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xinyue Zhao: Validation.
